title: Properties
summary: Use @property to expose methods like attributes â€” computed values and validated setters, without changing how callers use your object.

blocks.md: Sometimes an attribute should be **computed** on the fly, or a value should be **validated** before it's stored. Python's `@property` lets a method be accessed like a plain attribute â€” no parentheses â€” so you can add logic later without breaking callers.

blocks.caption: a computed, read-only property
guard how a value is set

blocks.title: Start simple, upgrade later
blocks.md: Begin with a plain attribute (`self.radius = radius`). If you later need validation or computation, turn it into a `@property` â€” callers still write `obj.radius`, so nothing else changes. This is why Python doesn't need Java-style getters/setters up front.

blocks.question: How do you access a value defined with @property?
options: obj.area â€” like a plain attribute, no parentheses
blocks.explanation: A @property is accessed like an attribute (obj.area) â€” the method runs behind the scenes. That's the point: it looks like data but can compute or validate.

keyTakeaways: `@property` turns a method into a read-only attribute accessed without ().
Add a matching `@<name>.setter` to validate or transform values on assignment.
Store the backing value in a `_name` attribute by convention.
You can convert a plain attribute to a property later without changing calling code.

exercise.prompt: Give a `Temperature` class (storing `self.celsius`) a read-only `@property` called `fahrenheit` that returns `celsius * 9/5 + 32`.
exercise.hint: Decorate a method named fahrenheit with @property and return self.celsius * 9/5 + 32.
